sentences.text: Good morning.
My name is Henry Blasenstein, B-L-A-S-E-N-S-T-E-I-N.
Yes.
I was born on-- in Bochnia, B-O-C-H-N-I-A, in Poland, in 1925, October the 29, 1925.
[SIGHS] Well, we used to live together with our grandparents in one house.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father, he-- I don't know him-- I don't remember him too well because he left when I was very young, maybe three, four years old.
So I lived with my mother and my grandparents, an uncle, and my sister.
And we had a-- a lumber yard and heating materials.
And I went to a public school and a high cheder.
And I made seven-- seven years public school.
And that's when the war started in 1939.
My name-- or my mother's name was Dora Blasenstein.
Maiden's name was Wanderer.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She was a housewife.
She didn't work, naturally.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she raised her two children.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know what to-- what I can say more about her, you know.
Well, she was a very good mother.
And I'm sure she-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she had her own problem because of her marriage.
But I really don't remember what was the problem then, because I was very young.
I know-- know one thing.
They were not divorced, but they were separated.
And he went back to Czechoslovakia because he was-- he was from Czechoslovakia, from [INAUDIBLE].
And I remember he used to write maybe once a year.
And-- and I am sure she felt very bad about it.
Because the problem in Poland in [INAUDIBLE] families, especially the family was well-to-do, is the marriages were not love marriages.
But the grandfather, he was the one who set the-- the marriage.
Let's say you have to, what they say in Jewish, a shidduch.
And-- and I am sure that's why their marriage didn't work, because they were not matched to each-- with each other or to each other.
And she never remarry, naturally.
And that's what I remember about her, you know.
And when the war started-- it was in 1939-- we moved already out from our house.
We had to go to another house.
Mhm, yes.
Grandparents and a uncle.
Oh, they are very nice people, very lovely people.
He-- he-- he always was saying, I would like to live to be on your-- [CRYING] Sorry.
[CRYING] He would say he would like to live to be on my bar mitzvah.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SOBBING] But he didn't.
[CRYING] He died three years before.
And so my grandmother.
She died four years before my bar mitzvah.
[CRYING] We lived also together with our uncle.
He was single.
He was not married.
But we were all together in-- in the business.
That was my mother's brother, yes.
He was her older brother.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] His name was Leon, Leon Wanderer.
Oh, we got very well.
I remember we were-- very well life.
Whole family got very well.
Yes, I have one sister.
She was two years older from me.
Her name was Emili, Emilia.
Well, we got very-- we got well along.
You know, sometime-- you know how it is between a brother and sister.
You may sometime have some-- may not like, you know-- or something about them.
But we were-- we got along very well.
My grandfather was a very religious man.
He was a man-- man with a long white beard.
You know, we lived in-- in-- in southern Poland, what they call Galicia.
When it came Sabbath, he used to wear what you call a shtreimel.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he used to wear a-- a long satin-- satin, long-- like a capote, you know.
A-- a coat, you know.
[SIGHS] I don't know how to explain it in English.
You can see that, you know, in Borough Park, what they're wearing.
It comes on the Sabbath.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
It's make-- it's a fur hat.
It makes from fur.
Mhm.
A-- a round one, you know?
And on top, it's made from satin.
But all around there, it's made from fur.
That's what they use to wear it-- in the Sabbath and on the Jewish holidays.
Well, I used to go with him to temple every Saturday.
He-- he used to live like on outskirts of the city.
So we had a long walk-- walk to go to-- to temple in-- in the town, in that city.
So I always walked with him to-- I had to go together with him.
And then, when you came home, you had the Sabbath meal what has to consist, you know, of fish and-- you know, a Sabbath meal, a-- a chicken soup and-- and all kind of, you know, holiday food.
And most of the time, we always brought home from the temple a man who was, you know, poor, who needed a-- a-- a meal.
So he used to come with us home, you know.
And he had a meal with us together.
[CRYING] Was this a different person every-- every week?
Or--
There used to come people from-- from other part of Poland, you know.
And it-- it's not-- it's not exactly that they were poor.
But they didn't have where to be-- where to have a meal for-- for Sabbath.
So we used to take a-- always a man for-- for our Sabbath meal.
Mhm.
Well, there was a lot anti-Semitism.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Many time you were beat up during recess, you know, in the school's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yard, you know.
And you always-- you-- you couldn't attend together with all the other Polish kids' plays or something because they would beat you up or something.
So most of the time I was standing, like, in a corner, against a wall or something, you know.
And after recess, you went back to-- to school.
And-- and in Poland, you see, that was-- when there was religion, we had to leave, to stay in the corridors when they had religious classes, you know.
And the church was not friendly to Jews, you know, in Poland, like it is now, you know.
And we used to have religion separate, I think.
Once a month, it used to come a-- a Hebrew teacher or a religious teacher.
And we had afternoon, in school for an hour, religion classes.
Yes, that I-- I attended after noon.
And about two or three times a-- a week, I used to go for a couple of hours to go to a cheder, yes.
Well-- [SIGHS]-- I was not a good student, let's put it this way.
It didn't-- didn't go into my head.
It wasn't a-- a very religious cheder, you know.
Just we learned the main thing, you know, about-- about the religion, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So, you know, I went in cheder just maybe for three, four years because then the war started, you know.
When I-- when I was smaller, I don't remember to go there, to cheder, because as I said before, we used to live like in an outskirts of-- of the city.
So somebody would have to walk to me-- with me to the-- to the city to attend the cheder.
How far a walk?
But I-- well, it was-- oh, yeah.
I would say it would be about 2 kilometers.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
So I didn't have too much religious upbringing.
But I spoke perfect Jewish and perfect Polish, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] as my whole family.
My grandfather spoke perfect Polish too.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we spoke-- at home, we spoke Polish and we spoke Jewish.
Well--
Well, I was a very good student in Poland.
You know, when I used to get mine report card, there was what-- one line, from top to bottom, what it says in Polish, 'bardzo dobrze.' That means, very good.
I was very good in-- in-- in-- in every subject in the school.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I supposed to go-- after that, supposed to go-- because Bochnia was a small town.
It was about 15,000 inhabitant.
So I supposed to go to Krakow to attend an-- what they call 'gymnasium' in Polish, in [INAUDIBLE]-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] high school, you know.
But even-- they had a high school in-- in-- in Bochnia too.
But the war broke out, and everything ended.
I was not yet 14 years old.
Well, they were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] depends.
Sometimes they were good.
I remember one accident.
We used to have a-- we used to have a big house, you know.
And one time, in the middle of the night, we heard the-- the windows were broken.
A stone fell through-- through the bedroom.
And-- and it fell not far from-- from-- from the bed where my mother was sleeping.
And, you know, in the beginning, you are afraid to walk to the window because you don't know what's happened.
But finally, the following day, we found out.
You see, Bochnia was a town-- salt-- salt mines.
So the workers what were going home in the middle of the night from the-- from the night shift or whatever, they got drunk.
And they just throw rocks, you know.
It must be for fun or whatever, you know.
And I remember, the police came the following day.
And they tried to smooth it that we shouldn't make no complaints, you know.
They didn't wanna do it.
They did it just because they were drunk, you know.
And-- and I don't think so they-- they did anything against them.
And I imagine there must be other-- other times when there must have have some problems.
But I wouldn't remember, you know.
I was-- I was too young for that.
Hm.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Well, I imagine my worst thing was when my grandparents passed away, because they were like [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the closest family, you know.
He-- my grandfather was like my father, you know, because I was brought up by them, you know, in one house.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
And I-- and I miss them very much.
How about a happier time?
Well, we used to-- I used to have six uncles and three aunts.
[PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] And I remember, during the summer, I used to go for vacation spent by them.
And they used to send their children, the cousins, what used to come to spend the summer by us.
So that was happy times.
Oh, they live all-- all around Poland.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There's-- there's one who lived in the same town, in Bochnia, where I was.
One lived in Krakow.
One lived in Tarnow.
One lived in [INAUDIBLE].
And I had a uncle, too, who emigrated to-- to Brazil.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I had aunts who lived in Tarnow and one in Szczuczyn.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Actually, there was-- there was two who lived in Tarnow and one in Szczuczyn.
My-- my mother was the third one.
There were-- my grandfather had nine-- actually he had 11 children, by two passed away what I never knew them.
So when the-- board work out, they-- I had six uncles and three-- actually, two aunts, because my mother was the third one from [INAUDIBLE].
And I have [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at least about 15 cousins because every one of them had two or three children.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Well, I remember it very well.
That was the-- the 3rd of September.
It was on a Friday.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We knew that the Germans are getting closer and closer.
And I remember we were in our next-door apartment together with-- with neighbors.
And we spent all night there, sitting and waiting for-- for arrival of the German.
And we heard the-- the-- the bombardments and-- and-- all night long.
And by daybreak, they came in-- into town.
And naturally, everybody was afraid to-- to-- to go out from-- from the home.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's from the beginning when it start to have the problems with the Jews.
We had to start to wear a-- white armbands with the-- with the name 'Juden' on it.
There was no more school, no more nothing.
I was less than 14 years.
And I just-- I was tall and skinny, so they took me right away to work.
And that was like winter time.
So we used to have to-- we had to [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] clean the streets from snow, you know.
It was organized by a Judenrat.
They organized-- right away, they-- they organized a Judenrat. 'Judenrat' means [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they selected a few of the Jews.
And they're supposed to organize the-- the work force.
And-- and they were responsible for-- for-- for-- for the rest of the Jews in the town.
They were the eldest from-- from on the town.
They selected most everybody, you know.
They-- they selected all the people.
They found all kind of jobs for them, you know, to go to work.
And so I remember I was-- had to go every day and-- and load, you know, snow on trucks, you know.
And-- because winters in Poland are a lot snow.
And the snow comes down in-- in September and October.
It stays till-- till-- till April and May, you know.
They have very severe winters there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and that's how it went for the next-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was '39-- for the next two years.
Oh, typical day there was you had to get up in the morning.
You had to be 6 o'clock in the morning in that Judenrat, you know.
And that's where they distributed work for-- for-- for people, you know.
They organized this group.
This 10 people goes to this place.
And the other 10 people go to another place, you know.
And-- and all depended-- depended how many people that day the Germans needed.
Let's say they gave an order to the Judenrat.
I need today 50 people, 100 people, or whatever, you know.
So they had to-- to give them the-- the 100 people, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If they-- if they didn't have them-- so they used to go in and-- and knock on the door and say, you have to go to work because we need-- we need the people to go to work.
[SIGHS] Well, we started like 7 o'clock till-- till we finished the-- the-- the assigned work.
That could take till-- till 4:00 or 5:00 or 6:00, you know.
Depends how much snow that day we had to collect or-- or do other jobs, you know.
Yes.
No.
Your uncle, for example.
My-- my uncle.
I think he-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] now I have to go back.
My uncle, by that time, he was married.
So he lived someplace else.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he had a-- a baby by that time already.
He married, I think, in 1938.
So he had his own apartment in that city.
So he-- he was, like, not home.
And what he was doing-- I tell you the truth-- I don't know.
He must've been [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe doing a job or something what-- what the German needed or something, you know.
And my mother was with my sister.
They were home.
Women they didn't take to work.
Yes.
Uh-huh.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can tell you we used to live that time-- I can tell you one-- one-- one time what happens.
We used to live that time in the middle of that city in the-- like a marketplace.
And right next door to us was the-- the main commander of the SS.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And during the night-- I'll never forget that.
That was in our Saturday night.
We had shooting and screaming and yelling.
And that was like right next door to us, you know.
If we would-- we were afraid to walk over to the windows because it might-- we could maybe see what's going on.
But we were frightened.
In the morning when we woke up, right in front of our house were three people hanging on our lamppost.
But they were not Jews.
They were three Poles.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I still don't remember.
I think they were drunk, the Poles.
And-- and the Germans were drunks.
And-- and they hanged them, you know.
And that's how they were hanging for maybe 10 days under the lamppost.
And that was the-- the first impression, you know, from German.
Then they start, you know, to close down all the Jewish stores.
